nativeRange.habitat: Rubble and dead reef areas of outer slopes and inshore zones, typically found in small groups along reef-to-sand boundaries at depths of roughly 3-15 m.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on zooplankton and benthic algae in the wild. In captivity, generally accepts a varied diet of mysis, brine shrimp, finely chopped marine meats, herbivore preparations with spirulina, and quality flakes or pellets fed two to three times daily.

summary: A small, vivid electric-blue damselfish from the eastern Indian Ocean, commonly sold as Neon, Andaman, or Blue Star Damsel. Generally hardy and beginner-friendly, but tends to become territorial as it matures.
careNotes: Allen's Damselfish is one of the more widely sold beginner-friendly marine fish and is commonly considered reef-compatible, though some sources note it may pick at benthic invertebrates and small shrimp as it matures — best treated as reef-safe with caution rather than universally reef-safe. Sources consistently describe it as semi-aggressive, with Quality Marine noting individuals can become more combative with age, particularly toward congeners or similarly shaped fish; LiveAquaria and BlueZoo treat it as semi-aggressive overall. Although the species is small (typically 5-6 cm, occasionally a little larger), generally advised tank minimums of 30 US gal (~114 L) are widely cited; biasing toward a ~120 L footprint with ample rockwork and open swimming space tends to reduce conspecific and inter-species aggression as the fish matures. Best kept singly per tank unless attempting a juvenile shoal in a notably larger system. The common name 'Neon Damselfish' is also applied to Pomacentrus coelestis in some sources, so verify scientific names when purchasing. Lifespan was not stated by the sources reviewed; damselfishes in this size class are generally reported to live several years in well-kept aquaria.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pair bonding during breeding. Females deposit demersal eggs that adhere to the substrate, with males providing parental care by guarding and aerating the nest. Captive breeding is rarely attempted and rearing the pelagic larvae is generally considered difficult.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Damselfish collection page lists Allen's Damselfish. Detailed product page (Neon Damselfish / Pomacentrus alleni) gave husbandry baseline: 30 US gal minimum, 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12, semi-aggressive, reef compatible, omnivore, max 2.5 in, easy care.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing and temperament: 2.5 in, 30 gal min, semi-aggressive, reef compatible, omnivore, beginner care, native range Andaman Sea / Fiji.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy authority: Pomacentridae, Ovalentaria/misc, max 6.0 cm TL, depth 3-15 m, wild temperature 28.1-29.4°C, distribution Similan Islands (Thailand) and Indonesia. Oviparous with male egg-guarding behavior.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Sold as 'Blue Star Damselfish'. Husbandry: 30 gal min, 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12, max ~3 in, becomes aggressive/territorial with age, may pick at benthic invertebrates so not fully reef-recommended. Disagrees with LiveAquaria/BlueZoo on reef-safety strictness — final value 'with caution' reflects the spread.

fish.breedingNotes: Pairs spawn on a cleaned substrate; males guard and fan the demersal eggs. Larvae are pelagic and rarely reared in captivity.
